/lessons/gender-nouns-1: In Spanish, all nouns have a grammatical gender: they are either masculine or feminine.
While there are exceptions, the most common way to identify the gender of a noun is by its ending:
Always learn the noun with its article (el or la). This is the most reliable way to remember its gender.
/lessons/gender-nouns-2: Beyond -o and -a, there are other common endings that can help you determine a noun's gender.
Nouns ending in these suffixes are almost always feminine:
Nouns of Greek origin ending in
are usually masculine, even though they end in -a.
/lessons/plural-nouns: Making nouns plural in Spanish is a straightforward process based on whether the word ends in a vowel or a consonant.
If a noun ends in a vowel, simply add
If a noun ends in a consonant, add
/lessons/articles: Spanish articles must agree in gender and number with the nouns they modify.
/lessons/subject-pronouns: Subject pronouns are the words that tell us who is performing the action of the verb.
In many parts of the Spanish-speaking world,
is used for both formal and informal 'you all,' and
/lessons/regular-verbs-1: In Spanish, verbs are categorized by their endings: -ar, -er, or -ir. This lesson focuses on the most common group: -ar verbs.
To conjugate a regular -ar verb in the present tense, remove the -ar to find the stem, then add the following endings:
/lessons/regular-verbs-2: Regular -er verbs follow a pattern similar to -ar verbs, but using 'e' instead of 'a' in the endings.
To conjugate a regular -er verb in the present tense, remove the -er to find the stem, then add the following endings:
/lessons/regular-verbs-3: Regular -ir verbs are almost identical to -er verbs in their endings, with exceptions in the 'nosotros' and 'vosotros' forms.
To conjugate a regular -ir verb in the present tense, remove the -ir to find the stem, then add the following endings:
/lessons/adjectives-1: In Spanish, adjectives must agree in both gender and number with the nouns they describe.
Adjectives follow the same rules as nouns for making plural forms.
or a consonant don't change for gender, only for number.
el libro grande / la casa grande
/lessons/adjectives-2: In Spanish, the position of an adjective is just as important as its form.
Most adjectives follow the noun they modify, which is the opposite of English.
English: red car → Spanish: car red
Adjectives that indicate quantity or order often precede the noun.
In poetry or literature, adjectives may precede the noun to add emphasis or a descriptive quality, but in daily speech, they almost always follow the noun.
/lessons/ser-estar-1: In English, the verb 'to be' is always the same. In Spanish, there are two distinct verbs:
/lessons/ser-estar-2: Ser vs Estar II (Uses of Ser)
is used to describe permanent or essential qualities.
/lessons/ser-estar-3: Ser vs Estar III (Uses of Estar)
is used to describe temporary states, moods, and locations.
/lessons/ser-estar-4: can change the meaning of an adjective.
is for WHAT something is, and
is for HOW something is at the moment.
/lessons/negation: In Spanish, making a sentence negative is much simpler than in English. You just add
When answering a question in the negative, you often say
twice: once for the 'No' and once for the sentence negation.
/lessons/interrogatives: Spanish uses unique punctuation and specific question words to ask questions.
In Spanish, questions always start with an inverted question mark (
) and end with a standard one (
Notice that all question words in Spanish have an accent mark when used in a question.
/lessons/numbers-1: Counting is a basic skill in any language. Let's start with the first ten numbers in Spanish.
/lessons/numbers-2: As you go higher, Spanish numbers follow a pattern, especially after 20.
For numbers between tens, use 'y' (and) for 31 and above:
/lessons/telling-time: To tell time in Spanish, we use the verb
followed by the feminine article to agree with 'las horas'.
/lessons/dates: Talking about dates in Spanish follows a very specific formula.
While we use regular numbers for most dates, for the first day of the month, we use
Months in Spanish are not capitalized unless they start a sentence.
enero, febrero, marzo, abril, mayo, junio, julio, agosto, septiembre, octubre, noviembre, diciembre
/lessons/possessives-1: Possessive adjectives indicate ownership or relationship. In Spanish, they must agree in number with the noun they modify.
/lessons/possessives-2: For 'our' and 'your' (plural), the possessive adjectives also agree in gender.
/lessons/irregular-verbs-yo: Some common Spanish verbs are regular in all forms except for the first-person singular (yo).
/lessons/saber-conocer: Both verbs mean 'to know,' but they are used in completely different contexts.
Used for facts, information, and skills (how to do something).
Used for people, places, and things (being familiar with).
Both are irregular in the yo form:
/lessons/personal-a: When the direct object of a verb is a person, Spanish requires a special preposition: the personal
It doesn't translate to English, but it must be there if the object is human or a pet.
/lessons/gustar: doesn't mean 'to like' in the way English does. It literally means 'to be pleasing to.'
The thing being 'liked' is actually the subject of the sentence. Therefore, we mostly use only two forms:
I like coffee (Coffee is pleasing to me).
I like books (Books are pleasing to me).
You must use a pronoun with gustar to show who is pleased:
me, te, le, nos, os, les
/lessons/stem-changers-e-ie: Stem-changing verbs change their spelling in the root of the word for all forms except nosotros and vosotros.
These are often called 'boot verbs' because if you circle the changing forms on a standard grid, it looks like a boot!
/lessons/stem-changers-o-ue: Just like e->ie verbs, these verbs change the 'o' in the stem to 'ue' for all forms except nosotros/vosotros.
/lessons/stem-changers-e-i: A smaller group of -ir verbs change the 'e' in the stem to a simple 'i'.
/lessons/affinity-words: Spanish uses pairs of words to affirm or deny the existence of people or things.
In English, double negatives are avoided. In Spanish, they are required!
I don't see anyone (I don't see no one).
/lessons/por-para-1: In English, both 'por' and 'para' are often translated as 'for.' However, in Spanish, they have distinct uses that are never interchangeable.
as expressing movement through a space, duration of time, or cause/reason.
(through, along, by, or in the area of)
(because of, on account of, on behalf of)
/lessons/por-para-2: While 'por' is about the 'through' or the 'why,'
is about the 'for' in terms of destination, goal, or deadline.
as expressing a destination, a purpose, or a point in the future.
I study in order to learn (Purpose).
/lessons/adverbs: Adverbs describe how, when, or where an action occurs. Many Spanish adverbs are formed from adjectives.
To form an adverb from an adjective, use the feminine singular form and add
Adverbs that modify verbs usually come
the verb. Adverbs that modify adjectives or other adverbs come
/lessons/comparisons-inequality: In Spanish, we use specific formulas to compare things that are not equal (more than or less than).
Juan es más alto que Pedro.
I have less money than you.
Some adjectives have their own comparative forms:
/lessons/comparisons-equality: To compare things that are equal (as... as), Spanish uses different formulas depending on whether you are using adjectives or nouns.
Ella es tan inteligente como él.
She is as intelligent as him.
Notice that 'tanto' must agree in gender and number with the noun.
I have as many books as you.
There is as much people (as many people) as yesterday.
/lessons/superlatives: Superlatives are used to describe something that has the highest or lowest degree of a quality (the most, the least).
Juan es el más alto de la clase.
Juan is the tallest in the class.
Es la película menos interesante de todas.
It is the least interesting movie of all.
To say something is 'extremely' or 'very' [adjective], you can add the suffix
/lessons/pronouns-prep-objects: When a pronoun follows a preposition (like for, with, about), Spanish uses specific forms that are mostly identical to subject pronouns.
/lessons/direct-objects-1: Direct object pronouns replace nouns that receive the action of a verb, helping to avoid repetition.
In a simple sentence with one conjugated verb, the direct object pronoun must come
/lessons/direct-objects-3: Just like with infinitives, direct object pronouns can also be attached to present particles (-ando, -iendo).
Option 2: Attached to the end of the participle.
Notice that when you attach a pronoun to a present participle, you must add an accent mark to the original stressed vowel of the participle to maintain the correct stress.
/lessons/indirect-objects-1: an action is performed. Indirect object pronouns replace these nouns.
Similar to direct object pronouns, indirect ones usually come immediately
Juan gives the book to me.
/lessons/indirect-objects-2: can refer to many different people, Spanish often uses extra phrases for clarity.
I give the book to her.
In Spanish, the indirect object pronoun is often used even when the noun is also present in the sentence. This is perfectly normal and required in many contexts.
/lessons/indirect-objects-3: Just like direct object pronouns, indirect pronouns can also be attached to infinitives and present participles.
You always have the alternative option of placing the pronoun before the conjugated verb:
/lessons/pronouns-combined: When a sentence has both a direct and an indirect object pronoun, follow these two critical rules.
The indirect object pronoun always comes
He gives it to me. (me = indirect, lo = direct)
If both pronouns start with an 'l' (le/les + lo/la/los/las), the indirect pronoun changes to
/lessons/verbs-like-gustar: Many other verbs in Spanish follow the same 'backwards' structure as gustar.
/lessons/present-progressive: The present progressive is used to describe an action that is happening
I am speaking on the phone.
/lessons/irregular-participles: Some verbs have irregular present participles, often due to spelling changes or stem-shifts.
When the stem of an -er or -ir verb ends in a vowel, use
Only -ir stem-changing verbs keep their change in the participle.
/lessons/intro-preterite: The preterite tense is used to describe actions that were
in the past at a specific point in time.
Actions that were part of a chain of events.
Actions that occurred within a specific time frame.
If you see these words, you usually need the preterite:
/lessons/preterite-regular: To conjugate regular verbs in the preterite, remove the -ar, -er, or -ir ending and add these forms.
Notice that -er and -ir verbs share the same set of preterite endings!
/lessons/preterite-spelling: To preserve the original pronunciation of the infinitive, some verbs require a spelling change in the
These changes only happen in the first-person singular. All other forms follow the regular -ar pattern.
/lessons/preterite-stem-changers: verbs that change their stem in the present tense also change in the past.
/lessons/preterite-irregular-1: Some of the most common verbs in Spanish are completely irregular in the preterite. Interestingly, 'ser' and 'ir' share the exact same forms!
Ver follows the same pattern as 'dar', but with a 'v'.
/lessons/preterite-irregular-2: Many irregular preterite verbs follow a pattern: they have a special stem and share the same set of endings (which have no accent marks!).
/lessons/preterite-irregular-3: Continuing with the irregular stems that share the same endings (-e, -iste, -o, -imos, -ieron).
Poder: pude, pudiste, pudo, pudimos, pudieron
Poner: puse, pusiste, puso, pusimos, pusieron
Saber: supe, supiste, supo, supimos, supieron
In the preterite, some of these verbs take on slightly different meanings:
/lessons/preterite-irregular-4: The final group of irregular stems, including those that use a 'j' in their stem and have a slightly different plural ending.
For verbs with a 'j' in the stem (like decir), the third-person plural ending is
/lessons/imperfect-regular: The imperfect tense is used to describe ongoing, habitual, or descriptive actions in the past (was doing, used to do).
Just like in the preterite, -er and -ir verbs share the same endings in the imperfect.
In the imperfect, all -er/-ir endings have an accent mark on the 'i'.
/lessons/imperfect-irregular: irregular verbs in the imperfect tense.
Ver is only 'slightly' irregular because it keeps the 'e' from the infinitive (veía instead of vía).
/lessons/pret-vs-imp-1: Deciding between preterite and imperfect is one of the biggest challenges in Spanish. It depends on how you view the action.
Use the preterite for actions that are
or have a clear beginning and end.
Use the imperfect for actions that were
(I used to eat a lot as a child).
/lessons/pret-vs-imp-2: Often, both tenses appear in the same sentence to describe an action that was in progress when something else happened.
I was reading when Juan entered.
If two actions were happening at the same time, use the
/lessons/pret-vs-imp-3: Some verbs change their English translation depending on whether you use the preterite or the imperfect.
The preterite often focuses on the
of an action, while the imperfect focuses on the
/lessons/rel-pronouns-1: Relative pronouns are used to connect two sentences that share a common noun.
is the most common relative pronoun in Spanish.
Unlike in English, where you can often omit 'that' (the book [that] I read), in Spanish you
'Que' can refer to people or things.
It translates to: that, which, who, whom.
The book (that) I bought is good.
The woman who is speaking is my aunt.
/lessons/rel-pronouns-2: (plural) are used specifically for people, usually after a preposition.
Use 'quien' when referring to people after prepositions like
The woman whom you called is not here.
The friends with whom I travel are nice.
to mean 'what' or 'that which' when referring to an abstract idea, situation, or a previous clause.
Lo que necesito es tiempo. (What I need is time).
/lessons/formal-commands: Formal commands are used with people you address as 'usted' or 'ustedes' (strangers, superiors, groups).
/lessons/informal-commands-tu: Affirmative informal commands are used with friends, family, and people you know well.
form of the present tense (the él/ella form).
There are 8 common irregular commands in the 'tú' form:
/lessons/negative-commands: To tell someone NOT to do something, the rules change significantly, especially for the 'tú' form.
To form any negative command (Ud., Uds., or tú), start with the
For the 'tú' form, take the Usted command form and add
/lessons/commands-pronouns: The position of object pronouns changes depending on whether a command is affirmative or negative.
to the end of the verb.
Note: Adding a pronoun often requires adding an accent mark to maintain the original stress.
/lessons/reflexive-verbs-1: Reflexive verbs indicate that the subject performs an action on
Reflexive verbs are always accompanied by these pronouns:
In their infinitive form, reflexive verbs always end in
/lessons/reflexive-verbs-2: The placement of reflexive pronouns follows the same rules as direct and indirect object pronouns.
Many verbs in Spanish are reflexive even if they don't seem like an action performed on oneself in English.
/lessons/definite-article-2: In Spanish, the definite article (el, la, los, las) is used in several situations where it is omitted in English.
: El amor es importante. (Love is important.)
: El lunes voy al cine. (On Monday I'm going to the cinema.)
: El doctor García está aquí. (Dr. Garcia is here.)
(replaces possessive adjectives): Me lavo las manos. (I wash my hands.)
/lessons/possessive-pronouns: Possessive pronouns are used to show ownership without repeating the noun (mine, yours, theirs).
Possessive pronouns must agree in gender and number with the noun being replaced.
In Spanish, you usually include the definite article.
Tengo mi pluma y la tuya.
